text.title: video-generation-prompt-engineering Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for video-generation-prompt-engineering. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: For Runway Gen-3: use specific camera movement vocabulary (tracking shot, dolly, crane, orbiting), include motion speed descriptors (slow, fast, smooth), and use image input for style reference when character consistency matters.
rule.rationale: Runway's model was trained heavily on cinematic footage. Camera direction vocabulary directly controls the camera system. Image-to-video mode reduces variance compared to text-only generation.

rule.statement: For Luma Dream Machine: focus on subject and environment description, use emotional and sensory language, and minimize technical camera terminology. Luma responds better to descriptive scene prose than to cinematographic direction commands.
rule.rationale: Luma's training distribution favors naturalistic scene descriptions. Emotional and sensory language activates more consistent motion patterns than technical camera commands.

rule.statement: For Sora 2: write multi-subject and complex scene descriptions with temporal transitions (starting with X, gradually transitioning to Y). Sora understands complex interactions and can handle multi-stage prompts for extension workflows.
rule.rationale: Sora 2's architecture handles longer temporal sequences and complex scene physics. Temporal language activates its understanding of change over time within the clip.

rule.statement: For Veo 3: include audio and sound descriptions alongside visual content, and specify resolution (4K). Veo 3 is the only model that processes audio descriptions to generate synchronized audio tracks.
rule.rationale: Veo 3 uniquely generates native audio; audio descriptions activate this capability. Resolution specification triggers 4K output when available through Vertex AI.
